California has the highest total homeless population in the nation, which is driven by unaffordable housing, mental illness, substance abuse, and more.
There are currently more than 187,000 homeless people in California, both sheltered and unsheltered.
These people do not have housing stability, which is the ability to maintain which is the ability to access and maintain safe, consistent, and affordable housing.
Moreover, almost a fourth of all homeless Americans in California, including 44% of Americans that are chronically homeless.

Chronic homelessness is when someone with a disabling condition has been homeless continuously for a year or more or has had four episodes of homelessness in the past three years, which total over a year.

Currently, California's approach to solving homelessness is housing first, which helps homeless people access housing without requiring sobriety or treatment participation as a precondition while offering voluntary supportive services in the long-term supporting house of C housing category.
This program makes housing affordable with long-term rental assistance with low no limit on how young people can stay.
We believe that the best policy to address the homelessness crisis in California is long-term supportive housing combined with mental health and substance use resources provided.
We are going to supplement California's current approach of housing first with financial incentives for treatment because it improves housing stability and addresses chronic homelessness.

transcript.text: Every night in Santa Clara County, over 10,000 people face the reality of homelessness.
But the real issue isn't just the lack of shelter.
It's the lack of stability.
Current approaches often try to fix addiction or employment before housing.
But without a stable place to live, those solutions rarely succeed.

Our policy proposes that Santa Clara County prioritize funding housing centers for individuals experiencing chronic homelessness first and then expand funding to rehab facilities for those with illnesses.
This housing first approach focuses on creating stability before addressing other challenges.
The evidence is clear.
According to the US Department of Housing and Urban Development, about nine out of 10 people remain housed one year after receiving housing housing first assistance.
In addition, providing immediate housing increases engagement in job training, health care, and rehabilitation programs, leading to long-term success rather than temporary fixes.
By by prioritizing stable housing first and then addressing underlying issues, this policy offers a more effective, humane, and economically sustainable solution to homelessness in our community.

transcript.text: Um, in conclusion, the most effective way to address chronic homelessness in Santa Clar County is to prioritize a housing first approach because it creates stability, improves long-term outcomes, and reduces overall public cost.
As our group has demonstrated throughout this presentation, the core issue behind homelessness is not just the lack of shelter.
It is the lack of stability.
Without a consistent place to live, individuals cannot reliably maintain employment, access health care, or fully engage in rehabilitation programs.
Housing must come first in order for every other solution to work.
Our evidence strongly supports this claim.
According to the US Department of Housing and Urban Development, approximately 90% of indivi individuals remain housed one year after receiving housing first assistance.
This shows that when people are given stable housing without preconditions, they are significantly more likely to maintain that stability over time.

In addition, we discussed how housing first programs increase participate participation in job training, education, healthcare, and treatment programs directly addressing the argument that housing alone is not enough.
In reality, housing is what makes these other solutions possible.
Further research from the National Alliance to End Homelessness shows that supportive housing can reduce emergency room visits and hospitalizations by up to 40 to 60%.
Demonstrating that without stable housing, individuals are forced into costly short-term crisis systems rather than long-term recovery.

Even more importantly, studies comparing out comparing approaches show that housing first models consistently outperform treatment first or simultaneous approaches with significantly higher long-term housing retention rates, often nearly double those of traditional models.
This proves that trying to solve housing and rehabilitation at the same time is less effective because it ignores the reality that stability must come before successful treatment.
We also demonstrated that this policy is not only effective but economically responsible.

A systematic review of US housing first program found that for every $1 spent, communities receive about $1.80 in economic benefits due to reduced use of emergency healthcare, shelters, and law enforcement.
This connects directly to our earlier point that current systems relying on temporary shelters but and emergency responses are actually more expensive in the long run.
By shifting resources towards stable housing, the county can reduce long-term costs while improving outcomes.
Ultimately, this policy offers a realistic, evidence-based, and sustainable solution to to chronic homelessness.
It addresses the root cause of instability, builds on proven outcomes, and benefits both individuals and the broader community.
By prioritizing housing first, Santa Clair County can move beyond temporary fixes, and take a meaningful step towards ending chronic homelessness for good.
